content:
Psychological evaluation of children in custody cases has become a litigation weapon.

In the case the Supreme Court decided on June 11, the child was already seeing a therapist. She was also an alleged victim of sexual abuse under POCSO. 

Despite this, the Bombay High Court had appointed a four-member panel of experts to evaluate her.

4 people. To assess a child who was already in professional care.

The Supreme Court stepped in and changed this. 

The new rule is simple- evaluate the parents first. If the child genuinely needs to be assessed, one psychologist does it, not a panel. Minimum interaction. And only when absolutely necessary.

What I find most troubling is this- India has no data on how many children go through psychological evaluation in custody cases every year. 

No one tracks it. No one follows up on what repeated interviews do to a child over time. Courts order it and move on.

The Court's own words say it best- "the child cannot be treated as a mere evidentiary object."

But that is exactly what has been happening. 

Psychological evaluation in custody battles is rarely about the child's wellbeing. It is about the case. 

Both sides push for it because it creates pressure, causes delays, and gives them material to use in court. It is a strategy. And it works. So lawyers use it.

The Supreme Court has now put the child's welfare above the litigation. 

Every family court in India got that message on June 11. Whether they actually follow it is the real question.
